bytes.topic: Project Structure — MVC vs vertical slice, feature folders, layering conventions
bytes.title: Fiber Project Structure — Vertical Slice vs MVC and When to Use Each
bytes.body: Fiber doesn't enforce a project layout, so teams default to flat MVC: `routes/`, `handlers/`, `models/`, `services/`. This works until your app grows — then cross-cutting changes touch six directories for one feature. Vertical slice flips the axis: organize by domain, not layer. Each feature folder owns its handler, service, and repository. Register routes by calling a `Register(router fiber.Router)` function on each feature's package — `app.Group("/users", middleware.Auth())` passed into `users.Register()`. Use `fiber.New()` at the app root, mount feature groups via `app.Mount()` or `app.Group()`, and keep `main.go` as pure wiring. Middleware like `limiter`, `compress`, and `requestid` attaches at the app or group level, not buried in handlers. For monorepos, `internal/` enforces package boundaries — nothing outside your module imports your domain logic. Vertical slice pays off fast in Fiber apps: adding a `/payments` feature means one new folder and one `Register()` call, zero changes elsewhere.

bytes.topic: Routing — defining routes, route parameters, grouping and prefixing
bytes.title: Fiber Routing — Parameters, Groups, and Prefixes Done Right
bytes.body:
Fiber's router is built on fasthttp and uses a radix tree, making it one of the fastest in the Go ecosystem. Define routes directly on the app instance or scope them with `app.Group()` to share a prefix and middleware stack.

Route parameters use the `:param` syntax — access them via `c.Params("param")`. For optional segments, append `?`; for wildcards, use `*`. Fiber resolves conflicts deterministically: static segments beat parameterized ones, so `/users/me` always wins over `/users/:id`.

Grouping is where Fiber shines for API versioning. `app.Group("/api/v1")` returns a `fiber.Router` — mount middleware on the group and it applies only to those routes. Nest groups freely: a `/api` group can contain a `/users` subgroup with its own auth middleware. This keeps route registration declarative and co-located with the feature, not scattered across a monolithic route file. Use `app.Use()` on a group for prefix-scoped middleware without affecting global handlers.
bytes.code_snippet:
package main

import "github.com/gofiber/fiber/v2"

func main() {
    app := fiber.New()

    api := app.Group("/api")
    v1 := api.Group("/v1", authMiddleware) // prefix + middleware

    users := v1.Group("/users")
    users.Get("/", listUsers)           // GET /api/v1/users
    users.Get("/:id", getUser)          // GET /api/v1/users/:id
    users.Get("/me", getMe)             // static wins over :id
    users.Post("/", createUser)         // POST /api/v1/users

    app.Listen(":3000")
}

bytes.topic: Middleware — request pipeline, global vs route-specific, ordering matters
bytes.title: Fiber Middleware — Request Pipeline, Global vs Route-Specific, and Why Order Is Everything
bytes.body:
Fiber's middleware stack is a chain of `Handler` functions executed in registration order. Global middleware is registered with `app.Use()` before any route definitions — it intercepts every request. Route-specific middleware is passed as variadic arguments before the final handler, scoping it to that route only.

Order is deterministic and critical. If you register a rate limiter after your logger, the logger still runs on throttled requests. If you call `c.Next()` inside a middleware, execution passes to the next handler in the chain; omitting it short-circuits the pipeline entirely — useful for auth guards, harmful if accidental.

Fiber groups (`app.Group()`) let you apply middleware to a subtree of routes without polluting the global chain. A `/api/v1` group with JWT validation keeps public routes untouched. Middleware registered on a group runs before any group-level route handler, but after any global middleware already registered above it. Stack your high-frequency, low-cost middleware (logging, request ID) first; expensive middleware (auth, body parsing) only where needed.

bytes.topic: ORM & Database — query builders vs full ORMs, N+1 prevention, migrations
bytes.title: Fiber + GORM — Killing N+1 Queries and Managing Migrations the Right Way
bytes.body:
Fiber has no built-in ORM — you wire one in yourself. GORM is the dominant choice, but its convenience hides a classic trap: N+1 queries. When you call `db.Find(&posts)` then loop to fetch each post's author, GORM fires one query per row. In a Fiber handler returning 100 posts, that's 101 database round-trips.

Fix it with `Preload`: `db.Preload("Author").Find(&posts)` — GORM issues two queries total, joining results in Go memory. For join-heavy reads, `db.Joins("Author").Find(&posts)` pushes the join to PostgreSQL instead.

For query builders vs full ORMs: GORM wins for CRUD-heavy APIs wired into Fiber; `sqlc` (code-gen from raw SQL) wins when query control matters — no reflection overhead, compile-time safety, idiomatic Go structs returned directly into `c.JSON()`.

Never use `AutoMigrate` in production. Pair Fiber apps with `golang-migrate` or `goose` — version-controlled, reversible SQL files that run as part of your container startup or CI pipeline, not ad-hoc schema diffs.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — global error handlers, consistent error response format
bytes.title: Fiber Error Handling — Global Handlers and Consistent Error Responses
bytes.body:
Fiber's `ErrorHandler` config key lets you intercept every unhandled error in one place — no scattered `c.Status().JSON()` calls spread across handlers. Register it once in `fiber.Config` and every `c.Next(err)` or panicked fiber error lands there.

Fiber ships with `*fiber.Error` as its typed error. Use `fiber.NewError(code, msg)` to bubble domain errors up the call stack, and type-assert in your global handler to extract the status code. For unknown errors, default to 500.

Define a single `ErrorResponse` struct and always serialize through it. This guarantees your API never leaks raw Go error strings to clients and keeps frontend contracts stable. Pair it with Fiber's `app.Use(recover.New())` middleware — loaded before routes — to catch panics and forward them into the same pipeline.

Avoid defining per-route error formats. One handler, one shape, zero surprises.

bytes.topic: Authentication — JWT validation, session handling, auth middleware patterns
bytes.title: Fiber Auth Middleware — JWT Validation Without the Boilerplate
bytes.body:
Fiber's middleware chain lets you guard routes by attaching handlers before your business logic. The `gofiber/contrib/jwt` package wraps `golang-jwt/jwt` and plugs directly into this chain — no manual header parsing needed. Configure it with `jwtware.New(jwtware.Config{SigningKey: ...})` and mount it on a route group; any request with an invalid or missing `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header is rejected with 401 before your handler runs.

To propagate claims downstream, pull the token from `c.Locals("user")` (the key jwtware stores by default), type-assert to `*jwt.Token`, then cast `.Claims` to your custom struct. Avoid re-parsing the token inside handlers — the middleware already validated the signature.

For session-based auth, `gofiber/storage` adapters (Redis, Postgres, SQLite) back `gofiber/contrib/session`. Call `store.Get(c)` to retrieve or create a session, set typed values with `sess.Set("userID", id)`, and always call `sess.Save()`. Regenerate session IDs on login with `sess.Regenerate()` to prevent fixation attacks.

bytes.topic: Dependency Injection — built-in DI containers vs manual wiring, testability impact
bytes.title: Fiber DI — Manual Wiring vs. fx for Testable Route Handlers
bytes.body:
Fiber ships no DI container — and that's intentional. Handlers are plain `func(*fiber.Ctx) error` values, so wiring is done in Go itself.

The idiomatic pattern is struct-based handlers: define a struct holding your dependencies (DB pool, Redis client, logger), then attach handler methods to it. In `main.go`, construct the struct with real implementations; in tests, swap in mocks. No framework magic — just interface substitution.

Third-party containers like Uber's `fx` or `dig` layer constructor-based injection on top of this. `fx.Provide` registers constructors; `fx.Invoke` wires the Fiber app. This pays off in large codebases where dependency graphs are deep, but adds startup complexity and reflection overhead.

The testability tradeoff is real: manual wiring forces explicit interfaces at the seam — if `UserHandler` depends on `UserStore` (interface), swapping a mock in tests is one line. With `fx`, you test the same way, but the graph is assembled by the container, so integration tests require `fx.Options` overrides to substitute implementations.

For most Fiber services, start with struct-based manual wiring. Migrate to `fx` when constructor ordering becomes a maintenance burden.

bytes.topic: Input Validation — schema validation at the boundary, sanitization, error responses
bytes.title: Fiber Input Validation — Schema Validation and Structured Error Responses at the Boundary
bytes.body:
Fiber has no built-in schema validator, so validation lives in a thin layer between `c.BodyParser()` and your handler logic. The idiomatic approach pairs Fiber with `go-playground/validator`: define your DTO as a struct with `validate:` tags, parse the body, then validate before touching business logic. Never trust parsed data without it — `BodyParser` only decodes; it doesn't constrain values.

For sanitization, strip whitespace and normalize strings before validation, not after. Use a global `ErrorHandler` in `fiber.Config` to intercept `*fiber.Error` and validation errors centrally, returning a consistent `{ error, fields }` JSON shape instead of scattering `c.Status().JSON()` calls across handlers. Register the validator once as an app-level dependency and inject it via closure. This keeps handlers lean: parse → sanitize → validate → delegate. Any failure before delegation returns 400 with field-level detail, making client integration predictable and eliminating ambiguous 500s caused by unvalidated upstream data reaching the database layer.

bytes.topic: Testing — unit vs integration, mocking HTTP, test database strategy
bytes.title: Fiber Testing — Unit vs Integration, Mocking HTTP, and Test Database Strategy
bytes.body:
Fiber's `app.Test()` method is the cornerstone of its testing story — it accepts an `*http.Request` and returns an `*http.Response` without binding to a real port, making unit tests fast and isolated. For handler unit tests, construct your `fiber.App`, register only the route under test, and call `app.Test(httptest.NewRequest(...))`. For integration tests, wire the full middleware stack and a real test database — Fiber's middleware chain (CORS, JWT, rate limiter) must be exercised together to catch interaction bugs.

For mocking downstream HTTP calls, inject an `http.Client` into your service layer and swap it with a `httptest.NewServer`-backed client in tests — Fiber itself doesn't dictate this, but keeping clients injectable keeps handlers testable. For the database, prefer a real PostgreSQL instance via Docker (testcontainers-go) over SQLite shims; Fiber apps typically use pgx or sqlx directly, and dialect differences bite. Run `t.Cleanup()` to truncate tables between tests rather than rolling back transactions — it handles deferred side effects (triggers, audit logs) that rollbacks silently skip.
bytes.code_snippet:
func TestPostHandler(t *testing.T) {
	app := fiber.New()
	svc := NewPostService(testDB) // real pg testcontainer
	app.Post("/posts", NewPostHandler(svc).Create)

	body := strings.NewReader(`{"title":"Go Fiber"}`)
	req := httptest.NewRequest(http.MethodPost, "/posts", body)
	req.Header.Set("Content-Type", "application/json")

	resp, err := app.Test(req, -1) // -1 = no timeout
	assert.NoError(t, err)
	assert.Equal(t, fiber.StatusCreated, resp.StatusCode)
	t.Cleanup(func() { truncateTable(testDB, "posts") })
}

bytes.topic: Security — CORS, rate limiting, security headers, injection prevention
bytes.title: Fiber Security Stack — CORS, Rate Limiting, and Headers in One Setup
bytes.body: Fiber's middleware ecosystem lets you lock down an API in minutes. Use `limiter.New` from `github.com/gofiber/contrib/limiter` to throttle requests per IP — set `Max` and `Expiration` to control burst windows. For CORS, `cors.New` accepts an `AllowOrigins` slice and supports per-route mounting, so public endpoints and authenticated ones carry different policies. Security headers come via `helmet.New()`, which sets `X-Content-Type-Options`, `X-Frame-Options`, `Strict-Transport-Security`, and CSP in one call — no manual `c.Set` chains needed. Against injection, avoid `c.QueryParser` into raw SQL; always use parameterized queries via `pgx`. Fiber's `BodyLimit` middleware caps payload size, preventing large-body DoS. Stack order matters: place `limiter` before `cors` and `helmet` before your route handlers so every response — including errors — carries the right headers. Chain them globally on `app.Use` unless a route genuinely needs different behavior.

bytes.topic: Serialization — request/response DTOs, camelCase vs snake_case, null handling
bytes.title: Fiber Serialization — DTOs, camelCase vs snake_case, and Null Handling Done Right
bytes.body:
Fiber uses `encoding/json` by default, but struct tags are what actually control your wire format. When you define request/response DTOs, the `json:` tag governs field naming — use `json:"user_name"` for snake_case or `json:"userName"` for camelCase. Fiber's `c.BodyParser()` respects these same tags when deserializing incoming JSON, so your DTOs are symmetric in and out.

For null handling, lean on pointer types combined with `omitempty`. A `*string` field with `json:",omitempty"` is omitted entirely when nil — cleaner than sending `"field": null` to clients. If you need explicit nulls (e.g., PATCH semantics), keep the pointer but drop `omitempty`.

For high-throughput routes, swap the default encoder via `fiber.Config{JSONEncoder: sonic.Marshal, JSONDecoder: sonic.Unmarshal}` — Bytedance's sonic encoder is 3–5x faster than `encoding/json` and is a drop-in replacement. Your struct tags remain unchanged. Never call `json.Marshal` manually inside handlers; always go through `c.JSON()` so the encoder swap applies globally.

bytes.topic: Configuration — env vars, settings classes, secrets management
bytes.title: Fiber Configuration — Env Vars, Config Structs, and Secrets Done Right
bytes.body:
Fiber accepts a `fiber.Config` struct at initialization — use it to centralize app-level settings like timeouts, body limits, and error handlers rather than scattering them across handlers. Pair this with a typed `AppConfig` struct loaded from environment variables before `fiber.New()` is called.

Use `godotenv.Load()` in development to populate env vars from `.env`, but rely solely on real environment variables in production — never ship `.env` files. For secrets (DB passwords, API keys), read them once at startup into your config struct and never re-read from `os.Getenv` inside handlers; this avoids repeated syscalls and keeps secret access auditable in one place.

For validation, use the `validator` package on your config struct at boot — fail fast if `JWT_SECRET` is empty rather than panicking mid-request. Fiber's `fiber.Config.ErrorHandler` can then return clean JSON errors without leaking config values. Store sensitive fields as `string` (not logged types) and use `omitempty` with caution in marshaling paths to prevent accidental secret exposure.

bytes.topic: API Design — REST conventions, versioning strategy, OpenAPI generation
bytes.title: Fiber — REST Versioning, Route Groups, and OpenAPI Without the Boilerplate
bytes.body:
Fiber's `app.Group()` is the right primitive for API versioning — prefix once, inherit everywhere. Mount `/api/v1` and `/api/v2` as sibling groups so breaking changes land in v2 while v1 stays stable. Attach a deprecation middleware directly to the v1 group: set a `Deprecation: true` response header on every v1 response without touching individual handlers.

For OpenAPI, use `swag init` with `github.com/gofiber/contrib/swagger`. Annotate handlers with `// @Router /api/v2/users/{id} [get]`, `// @Param`, and `// @Success` comments, then mount `swagger.HandlerDefault` at `/swagger/*`. Fiber serves the Swagger UI with no custom HTML.

REST conventions that matter in Fiber: use `c.Status(fiber.StatusCreated).JSON(body)` on POST — never bare `c.JSON()`. Use `c.QueryInt("page", 1)` for pagination with a safe default. Return errors through a shared struct so clients get a consistent shape. Use `c.Locals()` to pass decoded JWT claims from auth middleware into handlers — avoid re-parsing the token per route.

bytes.topic: Performance — connection pooling, async handlers, response compression
bytes.title: Fiber Performance Tuning — Connection Timeouts, Async Handlers, and Compression
bytes.body:
Fiber's performance edge comes from Fasthttp's zero-allocation HTTP engine, but you leave gains on the table without tuning three key areas.

Connection management: Configure IdleTimeout (default unlimited) and ReadTimeout/WriteTimeout in fiber.Config to reclaim goroutines from stalled clients. For multi-core servers, Prefork: true spawns one process per CPU core using SO_REUSEPORT, sidestepping Go's scheduler overhead entirely — benchmark before enabling in production.

Async handlers: Fiber handlers run synchronously on Fasthttp's worker pool. For CPU-heavy or I/O-bound work, spawn a goroutine and drain results via a buffered channel to unblock the worker. Never access c.Locals() or c.Body() inside a goroutine — *fiber.Ctx is pooled and returned to the pool the moment the handler returns. Copy what you need before the go call.

Response compression: The built-in compress middleware supports gzip, deflate, and brotli. LevelBestSpeed cuts ~15% payload with negligible CPU cost; LevelBestCompression suits low-traffic static routes. Apply selectively — running it over already-compressed payloads (JPEG, WASM, protobuf) burns CPU with zero size reduction.

bytes.topic: Logging — structured logs, correlation IDs, request logging middleware
bytes.title: Fiber Logging — Structured Logs, Correlation IDs, and Request Middleware
bytes.body:
Fiber's `requestid` and `logger` middlewares combine to give you structured, traceable request logs with minimal setup. Register `requestid.New()` first — it injects a UUID into `c.Locals("requestid")`, accessible via `${locals:requestid}` inside the logger's format string.

The `logger.New()` middleware accepts a `Config.Format` string supporting built-in tokens: `${status}`, `${latency}`, `${method}`, `${path}`, `${ip}`, and any custom local via `${locals:key}`. Wrap those tokens in a JSON template to emit newline-delimited JSON access logs — no external library required.

For deeper integration, set `Config.Output` to any `io.Writer` — point it at a `zerolog.Writer` to merge access logs into your application's structured log stream.

Inside handlers, extract the correlation ID with `c.Locals("requestid").(string)` and thread it into downstream service calls or outgoing HTTP headers (`X-Request-ID`). Propagating the same ID across services lets you reconstruct distributed traces from logs alone — no dedicated tracing backend needed for simpler deployments.

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling — hot reload, code generation, scaffolding CLI
bytes.title: Fiber Dev Tooling — Hot Reload with Air and Scaffolding Your First App
bytes.body:
Fiber has no built-in hot reload, but the Go ecosystem's de facto solution is `air` — a live-reloading watcher that rebuilds your binary on file changes. Drop an `.air.toml` at your project root to tune build commands, watch directories, and exclude paths like `tmp/`. For Fiber specifically, set `cmd = "go build -o ./tmp/main ."` and `bin = "./tmp/main"` — air will kill and restart the process cleanly on each save, preserving your `fiber.New()` config.

For scaffolding, `gofiber/recipes` on GitHub is the official starting point — clone a recipe (REST API, JWT auth, websockets, etc.) rather than hand-rolling boilerplate. Pair this with `go generate` directives to automate repetitive code: embed `//go:generate` above your route groups to regenerate OpenAPI specs or mock handlers via `mockgen`. The `fiber/v3` CLI (currently experimental) adds `fiber new <app>` for opinionated project layout with middleware pre-wired. Together, air + recipes + go generate covers 90% of Fiber's dev-loop friction.
bytes.code_snippet:
# .air.toml — minimal Fiber hot-reload config
root = "."
tmp_dir = "tmp"

[build]
  cmd = "go build -o ./tmp/main ."
  bin = "./tmp/main"
  delay = 1000
  exclude_dir = ["tmp", "vendor", "testdata"]
  include_ext = ["go"]
  kill_delay = "500ms"

[log]
  time = true

bytes.topic: Background Jobs — task queues, scheduled jobs, worker process patterns
bytes.title: Fiber Background Jobs — Goroutines, Cron, and Graceful Shutdown
bytes.body: Fiber has no built-in job scheduler, so the idiomatic pattern is launching goroutines before `app.Listen()` and wiring their lifecycle into Fiber's `app.Hooks().OnShutdown()`. For scheduled work, pair Fiber with `robfig/cron`: instantiate the scheduler, register jobs, call `c.Start()`, then stop it cleanly on shutdown. For task queues, use a buffered channel as the queue and fan out to a worker pool of goroutines that drain it. Each worker receives a `context.Context` derived from a root context cancelled on shutdown — this propagates termination without leaking goroutines. Avoid spawning raw goroutines inside route handlers; instead, push work onto the channel and return immediately, keeping handler latency low. For one-off deferred tasks (e.g. post-request email), `app.Hooks().OnShutdown()` lets you drain the queue before the process exits, preventing silent job loss. Use `app.ShutdownWithTimeout(10 * time.Second)` to bound the drain window.

bytes.topic: Monitoring — health check endpoints, metrics exposure, distributed tracing
bytes.title: Fiber Observability — Health Checks, Prometheus Metrics, and OpenTelemetry Tracing in One Setup
bytes.body: Fiber's middleware ecosystem covers all three observability pillars with minimal boilerplate. For health checks, expose `/livez` (process alive) and `/readyz` (dependencies reachable — DB ping, cache check) as separate routes; Kubernetes probes treat them differently. For metrics, `fiberprometheus` auto-instruments every route with `http_requests_total`, `http_request_duration_seconds`, and in-flight gauges — mount it before your routes so no handler is missed. For distributed tracing, `otelfiber` from `gofiber/contrib` injects a span per request and propagates W3C `traceparent` headers downstream, letting you correlate Fiber traces with calls to gRPC services or external APIs in tools like Jaeger or Tempo. One critical detail: call `otelfiber.Middleware()` before `fiberprometheus` so trace IDs are available on the span when metrics are recorded. Use `fiber.New(fiber.Config{DisableStartupMessage: true})` in production to avoid stdout noise that pollutes structured log pipelines. All three integrations add under 2ms p99 overhead on commodity hardware.

bytes.topic: Framework-specific — Django batteries-included, FastAPI auto-docs, NestJS decorators, Phoenix LiveView
bytes.title: Fiber's Built-In Middleware Stack — Production-Ready Without Extra Dependencies
bytes.body:
Fiber ships with 20+ production-grade middleware packages under `github.com/gofiber/fiber/v2/middleware/` — no third-party installs needed. Unlike raw `net/http` where you bolt on every concern manually, Fiber's batteries cover logging, rate limiting, caching, compression, CORS, ETag, Helmet security headers, and more out of the box.

Each middleware is zero-config by default but accepts a typed config struct for tuning. `limiter.New()` uses an in-memory sliding window — swap the `Storage` field to a Redis-backed store with one line. `cache.New()` respects `Cache-Control` headers and deduplicates concurrent requests to the same key automatically. `logger.New()` outputs structured logs with a `Format` string using Fiber's own `${locals:requestid}` interpolation tokens — no external logger wiring required.

All middleware registers via `app.Use()` globally or scoped to a route group, so you can apply `limiter` only to `/api` without touching public asset routes. The composability is identical to Express.js middleware chains but with Go's type safety and near-zero allocation overhead from Fiber's `fasthttp` core.
